text: robotic transthoracic and transperitoneal esophagectomy on a da Vinci X system by Dr. Somshekhar SP, Head of the Department and Consultant Surgical Oncologist, Manipal Hospitals, Bangalore. A sixty-five-year-old male with ECOG of zero presented with dysphagia to solids more than liquid since one month There were no other complaints or comorbidities. Upper GI scopy revealed a three by three centimeter growth in the lower one third of the esophagus with lumen being partially compromised. A CT scan confirmed the findings with no evidence of metastasis. Biopsy revealed it to be a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma grade two. Hence, patient was given two cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy with TPF regime.

After the workup, the plan was to do a total esophagectomy with two-field lymphadenectomy including the transthoracic and transperitoneal approach using the da Vinci egg system. Reconstruction was planned using the gastric tube pull-up with cervical anastomosis. Intutu has an established procedure card for many surgical procedures on the da Vinci XI and SI system but for the esophageal transthoracic approach there were none. Hence we developed our own port placement technique for system X. Patient is given prone position with right arm completely hyper abducted and camera taken thirty degree down.

The robotic cart is docked from the left shoulder side at an acute angle to the table's long axis. The camera port is positioned two intercostal spaces below the tip of the right scapula. The midline of the spine is marked, and R1 port is placed seven centimeters from the midline, two to three intercostal spaces above the camera port, parallel to the medial border of the scapula. R2 port is positioned two spaces below the camera port, seven centimeters from the midline. The assistant port is triangulated between the camera and arm two.

The surgeon uses hot shears on the right and a vessel sealer on the left to dissect the infra-azygous part. The inferior pulmonary ligament is incised, allowing the lung to be repositioned downward. The esophagus is separated from the pericardium, and the subcarinal lymph nodes (station VII) are dissected, revealing the left main bronchus. The dissection continues along the pericardium using the hanging from roof technique, keeping the esophagus attached to facilitate further dissection, including the thoracic duct. The vagus nerve is visualized at the caudal aspect.
The surgeon visualizes the left pleura and continues the dissection caudally towards the lower gastroesophageal junction. The left crus of the diaphragm is brought into view.

The surgeon performs vagal nerve dissection below the tracheal bifurcation, revealing the left bronchial artery. The dissection progresses towards the supra azygous region, focusing on the recurrent laryngeal group of lymph nodes. The right recurrent laryngeal lymph nodes are meticulously dissected, with care taken to avoid injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerves.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the membranous trachea, using hot shears while avoiding iatrogenic injury. The assistant port is utilized to retract the trachea for safety. As dissection progresses cranially, the subclavian vein is visualized. The azygous vein is routinely ligated, and the esophagus is freed from the thoracic inlet to the hiatus.
